title: Cat Laborious Deep Breathing: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat has labored or deep breathing, it is a high-priority medical emergency. Learn the signs of respiratory distress and next steps.
content_html:
What is Laborious Deep Breathing in Cats?

Laborious breathing, medically known as dyspnea, occurs when a cat has to put in significant physical effort to move air in and out of their lungs. Unlike normal, quiet breathing, you may see your cat's chest heaving or their abdomen moving rhythmically to assist with respiration. This indicates that their body is struggling to maintain proper oxygen levels.

Is This an Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level is High. Cats are experts at masking illness, which means that visible respiratory distress is often a sign of a critical, life-threatening situation. If your cat is breathing with an open mouth, has blue or pale gums, or is standing with their elbows pointed outward to catch their breath, do not wait. Please transport them to the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

If you can do so safely without delaying your trip to the vet, taking a brief 10-second video of your cat breathing can be incredibly helpful for the medical team. Because cats often change their breathing patterns due to the stress of a car ride or a new environment, a video showing their 'at-home' respiratory effort allows the vet to see the severity and type of distress more accurately upon arrival.

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Breathing difficulties can escalate to respiratory failure very quickly. Any cat showing labored breathing requires immediate emergency intervention.

faqs.question: What are common causes of deep labored breathing in cats?
faqs.answer: Common causes include feline asthma, congestive heart failure (fluid around the lungs), pneumonia, or physical trauma.

faqs.question: Should I try to perform CPR if they stop breathing?
faqs.answer: Only attempt CPR if you have been trained, but your priority should be getting the cat to a facility with an oxygen cage and professional monitoring.
